senses.definition: relating to or causing procrastination; delaying action.
examples: Her procrastinatory habits made it hard to finish assignments on time.
A procrastinatory mindset can undermine productivity.

senses.definition: related to or engaged in reproduction or procreation.
examples: The procreant instincts of many species drive mating behaviors.
A procreant society faces ethical questions about population and family.

senses.definition: plural of procreant; individuals or entities that reproduce.
examples: The procreants in the population increased after the policy change.
Scientists studied how procreants in the species locate mates.

senses.definition: to reproduce or produce offspring.
examples: Animals procreate in the spring.
Humans have the right to procreate if they wish.

senses.definition: past tense of procreate; produced offspring.
examples: The couple procreated last year after marriage.
Many animals procreated to ensure species survival.

senses.definition: third person singular present of procreate; reproduces.
examples: Species procreates when conditions are favorable.
He worries that urbanization reduces opportunities to procreate.

senses.definition: present participle of procreate; engaging in reproduction.
examples: Many species are procreating after the rainy season.
Debates about population ethics often focus on procreating choices.

senses.definition: the act of producing offspring; the creation of life.
examples: Procreation is a natural part of many species' life cycles.
The meeting discussed ethics around procreation.

senses.definition: related to procreation or the act of creating offspring.
examples: The book discusses procreational ethics.
Procreational technology raises new questions.

senses.definition: offspring or the acts of creating offspring; products of creation related to reproduction.
examples: The species showed multiple procreations after mating.
Art and literature can be called procreations of the imagination.

senses.definition: relating to reproduction or the ability to produce offspring.
examples: Procreative energy is essential for species survival.
Some policies address procreative rights.

senses.definition: the quality of being creative in the sense of generating offspring or new creation; creativity related to reproduction.
examples: The procreativeness of the group was evident in their collaborative projects.
Exams praised students for procreativeness in problem solving.

senses.definition: the act or capacity of creating, especially in reproductive or generative contexts.
examples: Biologists study procreativity in various species.
Artistic procreativity can mirror biological processes.

senses.definition: a person or thing that creates offspring or origins; a source of creation.
examples: The couple was hailed as the procreator of their family line.
The author is seen as the procreator of a new literary genre.

senses.definition: plural of procreator; people or agents that create offspring or origins.
examples: Biologists compare procreators across species.
The term can refer to writers as procreators of ideas.

senses.definition: relating to the act of procreating or producing offspring.
examples: Procreatory instincts guide mating behaviors in many animals.
The debate covered procreatory rights and responsibilities.

senses.definition: historic or rare feminine form referring to a female procreator; often archaic.
examples: The term procreatress appears in older texts to denote a female parent.
In modern usage, procreator is common and gender-neutral.

senses.definition: historical or rare feminine form of procreator; gendered term.
examples: Procreatrix is an obsolete or rare form found in some texts.
Modern usage prefers neutral terms like procreator.

senses.definition: rare term; a portmanteau-like concept combining procreation and cremation; not standard in modern usage.
examples: Procremation is not a common term in current dictionaries.
In fiction, authors sometimes coin terms like procremation for world-building.

senses.definition: proper noun or mythological name (e.g., Procris in Greek myth); not a common English word outside references.
examples: Procris is a figure in Greek mythology.

senses.definition: a person who critiques proactively; not a standard dictionary entry; likely rare or nonce word.
examples: A keen procritic may offer constructive feedback early.

senses.definition: an act of critiquing in a procreative or forward-looking sense; not standard; nonce usage.
examples: The essay includes a procritique of societal norms.

senses.definition: rigidly or arbitrarily enforcing uniform standards; inflexible.
examples: The committee followed a procrustean rule that stifled creativity.
A procrustean approach ignores individual differences.

senses.definition: the practice or philosophy of imposing uniform, rigid standards on diverse cases; inflexibility.
examples: Critics condemned the policy as procrusteanism that suppressed diversity.
The novel critiques procrusteanism in administrative systems.

senses.definition: to impose rigid, uniform standards on diverse cases; to enforce conformity inflexibly.
examples: The administration attempted to procrusteanize all schools to the same testing regime.
Critics warned that such measures would procrusteanize creativity.

senses.definition: in mythology, a giant who forced guests to fit his bed by stretching or cutting them; used metaphorically for rigid conformity.
examples: The term procrustes is often used to describe any system that forces people to conform to a fixed standard.

senses.definition: relating to or resembling Procrustes; enforcing conformity; rigid.
examples: A procrustian critique often imposes one size fits all.

senses.definition: to enforce rigid conformity; to make things fit a fixed standard.
examples: The policy seeks to procrusteanize diverse workplaces.
Critics argued it would procrusteanize cultural expression.
